questions.question: Explain the purpose of the ios::binary flag when opening a file in C++. How does it affect data reading/writing compared to text mode? Provide an example of opening a file in binary mode.
keyPoints: ios::binary disables newline translation and treats file as raw bytes
In binary mode, data is read/written exactly as stored, without line‑ending conversion
In text mode, characters like Ctrl+Z may terminate reading

questions.question: Describe the functions seekg() and seekp() in C++ file handling. How are they used to move the read and write pointers respectively? Include a brief example showing how to move the pointer to a specific offset from the beginning of the file.
keyPoints: seekg(offset, ios::beg) moves the get (read) pointer
seekp(offset, ios::beg) moves the put (write) pointer
Offset can be positive or negative and the reference can be beg, cur, or end
Example: file.seekg(0, std::ios::beg); // reposition to start

questions.question: Write a C++ program that creates a binary file named 'student.dat' to store records of students (struct Student {int roll; char name[30]; float marks;}). The program should allow the user to: (i) add a new student record at the end of the file; (ii) display all records; (iii) update the marks of a student given its roll number using random access (seekg/seekp). Include proper error handling after file operations (check fail(), bad(), eof()) and ensure the file is closed using close().
keyPoints: Define Student struct, open file in std::ios::binary, use write() and read() with reinterpret_cast
For adding, open with ios::app, write record; for display, open with ios::in, read in loop, check eof()
For update, compute offset = (roll-1)*sizeof(Student), use seekg to position, then write updated record
After each operation check file.fail(), file.bad(), file.eof(); finally call file.close()

questions.question: A binary file 'ints.bin' stores 20 integers. Write a C++ program that asks the user for an index (0‑based) and a new integer value, then uses random access to replace the integer at that index. The program must check for errors using fail(), bad(), and eof() after reading/writing and must close the file properly. Also, demonstrate how to use seekg to position the read pointer to read a specific integer from the file.
keyPoints: Compute offset = index * sizeof(int), use seekp(offset, std::ios::beg) to position write pointer
Write new value using write(reinterpret_cast<const char*>(&newVal), sizeof(int))
For reading, use seekg(offset, std::ios::beg), read, and check fail(), bad(), eof(); close file with close()
